subject: Mathematics
solution:
Let A=1,2,3,4,5,.........,50

Now, the probability of choosing a number which is a multiple of 4 will be P4=1250

Similarly, the probability of choosing a number which is a multiple of 6 will be P6=850

And the probability of choosing a number which is a multiple of 7 will be P7=750

Also, the probability of choosing a number which is a multiple of 4 & 6 will be P4∩6=450

And the probability of choosing a number which is a multiple of 6 & 7 will be P6∩7=150

And the probability of choosing a number which is a multiple of 4 & 7 will be P4∩7=150

And finally, the probability of choosing a number which is a multiple of 4, 6 & 7 will be P4∩6∩7=0

Hence, P4∪6∪7=1250+850+750-450-150-150+0

⇒P4∪6∪7=2150

subject: Mathematics
solution:
n cards are drawn \& are found all spade, thus remaining spades $=13-\mathrm{x}$ remaining total cards $=52-\mathrm{x}$
Now given that $\mathrm{P}($ lost card is spade $)=\frac{11}{50}$
$\begin{aligned}
& \text { i.e. } \frac{{ }^{13-\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{C}_1}{{ }^{52-\mathrm{n}} \mathrm{C}_1}=\frac{11}{50} \\ & 50(13-\mathrm{n})=11(52-\mathrm{n}) \\ & 39 \mathrm{n}=78 \\ & \mathrm{n}=2
\end{aligned}$

& \therefore 126 P=54
\end{aligned}$

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Probability of getting sum of selected two numbers is even

$=P\left(E_{1}\right)=\frac{{ }^{5} C_{2}+{ }^{5} C_{2}}{{ }^{11} C_{2}}$

Probability of getting sum is even and selected numbers are also even $P\left(E_{2}\right)=\frac{{ }^{5} C_{2}}{{ }^{11} C_{2}}$

Hence, $P\left(\frac{E_{2}}{E_{1}}\right)=\frac{{ }^{5} C_{2}}{{ }^{6} C_{2}+{ }^{5} C_{2}}=\frac{10}{15+10}=\frac{2}{5}$.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Given,

Sum of two integer is 66, so one number will be x and other will be 66-x,

And given M is maximum value of their product,

So let y=x66-x

⇒y=66x-x2

Now differentiating to find maxima and minima we get, 

dydx=66-2x

Now equating with zero to find point of maxima as y=66x-x2 represents a downward parabola so it will give maxima,

So dydx=0⇒66-2x=0⇒x=33,

Hence, the value of M=33×33=1089

Now solving x66-x≥5M9

⇒x66-x≥5×10899

⇒x66-x≥605

⇒x2-66x+605≤0

⇒x-11x-55≤0

So x∈11,55→total 45 numbers,

Now for probability of A, favourable outcomes will be x=3k⇒x=12,15,18,.....54→ total 15 numbers,

So probability will be 1545=13

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Let, A be the event that the noted number is either 7 or 8.

And, E1 be the event that the toss of coin results in head and E2 be the event that the toss of coin results in tail.

Then, we have PE1=PE2=12

And, PA|E1=P(getting the sum of the numbers on the pair of dice as 7 or 8)

The total number of cases, when a pair of dice is thrown are =6×6=36 and the cases of getting sum 7 or 8 are 1, 6, 2, 5, 3, 4, 4, 3, 5, 2, 6, 1, 2, 6, 3, 5, 4, 4, 5, 3, 6, 2=11 cases.

⇒PA|E1=1136

Similarly, PA|E2=(getting the number 7 or 8 from the numbers 1, 2, 3,.., 9)

⇒PA|E2=29

Now, using the total probability theorem, we get

PA=PE1PA|E1+PE1PA|E2

⇒PA=12×1136+12×29

⇒PA=1172+19=1972.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Bag A: 9W, 8B. Bag B: 6W, 4B.
Case 1: White transferred from B (prob $= \frac{6}{10}$). A becomes 10W, 8B. $P(\text{white from A}) = \frac{10}{18} = \frac{5}{9}$.
Case 2: Black transferred from B (prob $= \frac{4}{10}$). A becomes 9W, 9B. $P(\text{white from A}) = \frac{9}{18} = \frac{1}{2}$.
Total probability $= \frac{6}{10} \cdot \frac{5}{9} + \frac{4}{10} \cdot \frac{1}{2} = \frac{1}{3} + \frac{1}{5} = \frac{8}{15}$.
$p = 8, q = 15, \gcd(8,15) = 1$. So $p + q = 23$.

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Since events A and B are mutually exclusive
$$
\begin{aligned}
&\therefore \mathrm{P}(\mathrm{A})+\mathrm{P}(\mathrm{B})=1 \\
&\Rightarrow \frac{3 x+1}{3}+\frac{1-x}{4}=1 \\
&\Rightarrow 12 x+4+3-3 x=12 \\
&\Rightarrow 9 x=5 \Rightarrow x=\frac{5}{9} \\
&\therefore x \in\left[-\frac{1}{3}, \frac{5}{9}\right]
\end{aligned}
$$

subject: Mathematics
solution:
Using Bayes' theorem:
$P(k=1|\text{all black}) = \frac{P(\text{all black}|k=1) \cdot P(k=1)}{P(\text{all black})}$ where $P(k) = \frac{1}{11}$ for each $k \in \{0,1,...,10\}$.
When $k=1$: $P(\text{all 3 black}|k=1) = \frac{\binom{9}{3}}{\binom{10}{3}} = \frac{84}{120} = \frac{7}{10}$.
Calculating $P(\text{all black}) = \frac{1}{11}\sum_{k=0}^{10} \frac{\binom{10-k}{3}}{120}$.
Numerator is $120 + 84 + 56 + 35 + 20 + 10 + 4 + 1 = 330$.
So $P(\text{all black}) = \frac{1}{11} \cdot \frac{330}{120} = \frac{1}{4}$.
Therefore $P(k=1|\text{all black}) = \frac{\frac{7}{10} \cdot \frac{1}{11}}{\frac{1}{4}} = \frac{7}{10} \cdot \frac{4}{11} = \frac{28}{110} = \frac{14}{55}$

X 0 1 2 3
PX 16 12 310 130

⇒σ2=12+1210+310-12+610+1102

⇒σ2=56100

⇒100σ2=56

solution:

Given n=7,

also , PX=3=5 PX=4 ⇒C37p3q4=5·C47p4q3

We know that p+q=1⇒6p=1 (from i)
